questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an accessor (getter) method in Java?
options: To modify a private field's value
To return the value of a private field without exposing the field directly
To validate input before storing it in a field
To make a field static so it can be accessed without an object
questions.answer: To return the value of a private field without exposing the field directly

questions.question: Which of the following is a correctly named mutator method for a field called 'email'?
options: public String getEmail()
public void email(String email)
public void setEmail(String email)
public String setEmail()
questions.answer: public void setEmail(String email)

questions.question: Why do accessor and mutator methods support encapsulation?
options: They allow fields to be declared public
They prevent the class from being instantiated
They control access to private fields and allow validation of changes
They automatically synchronize access across multiple threads
questions.answer: They control access to private fields and allow validation of changes

questions.question: A class has a private field 'price'. The setter should reject negative values. Which implementation is correct?
options: public void setPrice(double price) { this.price = price; }
public void setPrice(double price) { if (price < 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Price cannot be negative"); this.price = price; }
public double setPrice(double price) { return this.price = price; }
public static void setPrice(double price) { price = price; }
questions.answer: public void setPrice(double price) { if (price < 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("Price cannot be negative"); this.price = price; }

questions.question: When should a field have a getter but NO setter?
options: When the field is declared public
When the field is a static variable
When the field is read-only, such as a final field set in the constructor
When the field holds a String value
questions.answer: When the field is read-only, such as a final field set in the constructor

questions.question: What does the 'static' keyword mean when applied to a method?
options: The method can only be called once
The method belongs to the class rather than to any specific instance
The method cannot have parameters
The method is automatically called when the class is loaded
questions.answer: The method belongs to the class rather than to any specific instance

questions.question: How should a static method be called?
options: By creating an instance and calling object.method()
By using the class name, such as ClassName.method()
By importing the method into the main class
By declaring the method inside the main method
questions.answer: By using the class name, such as ClassName.method()

questions.question: Why can a static method NOT use the 'this' keyword?
options: The 'this' keyword is deprecated in modern Java
Static methods run before any objects exist, so there is no current instance to refer to
The 'this' keyword is only available in constructors
Static methods can use 'this' but only with special syntax
questions.answer: Static methods run before any objects exist, so there is no current instance to refer to

questions.question: Which of the following is a well-known static method from the Java standard library?
options: "hello".length()
Math.sqrt(25)
scanner.nextInt()
list.add("item")
questions.answer: Math.sqrt(25)

questions.question: A static method in a class tries to access an instance variable directly. What happens?
options: The code compiles and runs normally
The code compiles but throws a NullPointerException at runtime
The code fails to compile with a 'non-static variable cannot be referenced from a static context' error
The code compiles but the instance variable returns its default value
questions.answer: The code fails to compile with a 'non-static variable cannot be referenced from a static context' error

questions.question: Which type of method should be used when the method does NOT need any instance data and performs a general utility operation?
options: An instance method
A static method
A constructor
An abstract method
questions.answer: A static method

questions.question: Why must the 'main' method in Java be declared as static?
options: Because main methods cannot accept parameters
Because the JVM needs to call it without creating an instance of the class first
Because main is a reserved keyword that requires static
Because main must return an integer exit code
questions.answer: Because the JVM needs to call it without creating an instance of the class first

questions.question: Why do utility classes like StringUtils typically have a private constructor?
options: To allow subclasses to override the constructor
To improve the performance of static method calls
To prevent instantiation since all methods are static and no object is needed
To make the class abstract without using the abstract keyword
questions.answer: To prevent instantiation since all methods are static and no object is needed

questions.question: Consider a class GameCharacter with a static field 'totalCharacters' and an instance field 'name'. Which statement is true?
options: Each GameCharacter object has its own copy of totalCharacters
The totalCharacters field is shared by all instances of GameCharacter
The name field is shared by all instances of GameCharacter
Static fields cannot be of type int
questions.answer: The totalCharacters field is shared by all instances of GameCharacter

questions.question: What is the correct return type for a getter method that returns a boolean field called 'isActive'?
options: void
String
boolean
int
questions.answer: boolean

questions.question: A getPassword() method returns '********' instead of the actual password. What principle does this demonstrate?
options: Polymorphism
Encapsulation and data hiding — the getter controls what information is exposed
Inheritance — the password class overrides the return value
Abstraction — the password does not actually exist
questions.answer: Encapsulation and data hiding — the getter controls what information is exposed

questions.question: Given: static int totalBattles = 0; Two different objects each call a method that increments totalBattles by 1. What is the value of totalBattles after both calls?
options: It depends on which object called it last

questions.question: Which of the following can a static method access?
options: Instance fields and instance methods only
Static fields and static methods only
Both static and instance fields and methods
Neither static nor instance fields
questions.answer: Static fields and static methods only

questions.question: Which of the following can an instance method access?
options: Instance fields and instance methods only
Static fields and static methods only
Both static and instance fields and methods
Only fields passed as parameters
questions.answer: Both static and instance fields and methods

questions.question: A setter for 'username' requires the name to be 3-20 characters, start with a letter, and contain no spaces. Which input would pass all validation checks?
options: "ab"
"John Doe"
"player1"
"3rdUser"
questions.answer: "player1"

questions.question: What exception type is most appropriate to throw when a setter receives an invalid argument?
options: NullPointerException
ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
IllegalArgumentException
ClassCastException
questions.answer: IllegalArgumentException

questions.question: A static method compareInventories(Inventory a, Inventory b) compares two Inventory objects. Why is this method static rather than instance?
options: Because it takes parameters
Because it does not operate on a single instance's data — it compares two separate objects at the class level
Because it returns void
Because static methods are faster than instance methods
questions.answer: Because it does not operate on a single instance's data — it compares two separate objects at the class level

questions.question: A field is declared as 'private final String accountId' and assigned in the constructor. Which methods should this field have?
options: Both a getter and a setter
Only a setter
Only a getter, because a final field cannot be reassigned after construction
Neither a getter nor a setter
questions.answer: Only a getter, because a final field cannot be reassigned after construction

questions.question: What is the result of calling StringUtils.truncate("Hello World", 5) if the method truncates to maxLength and appends '...' when the original string is longer?
options: "Hello World"
"Hello..."
"Hello"
questions.answer: "Hello..."

questions.question: A class ArrayUtils has a static method 'sum(int[] arr)'. What should this method do if the array passed to it is null?
options: Return 0 silently
Return -1 as an error code
Throw an exception or handle the edge case with validation
Access the array anyway and let the JVM handle it
questions.answer: Throw an exception or handle the edge case with validation
